trans-unit.source: Symbology and Colours
trans-unit.target: Symbology and Colours

trans-unit.source: The representation of geodata in maps is essential for providing useful location-based insights. This subchapter will cover the basics of good map design, how to create a map design in QGIS, as well as common mistakes when designing or interpreting maps.
trans-unit.target: The representation of geodata in maps is essential for providing useful location-based insights. This subchapter will cover the basics of good map design, how to create a map design in QGIS, as well as common mistakes when designing or interpreting maps.

trans-unit.source: In this chapter we will go over the basics of symbology, colours, and how to adjust individual layers in QGIS to create comprehensive maps.
trans-unit.target: In this chapter we will go over the basics of symbology, colours, and how to adjust individual layers in QGIS to create comprehensive maps.

trans-unit.source: ::::{admonition} Recap: Types of Maps :class: seealso
trans-unit.target: ::::{admonition} Recap: Types of Maps :class: seealso

trans-unit.source: In general, there are two main types of maps: __topographic maps__ and __thematic maps__.
trans-unit.target: In general, there are two main types of maps: __topographic maps__ and __thematic maps__.

trans-unit.source: __Topographic maps__ are intended to be exhaustive, including elements fundamental to localisation (localities, road networks, terrain, hydrography). They display the physical location of objects in the real world. The representation of elements in topographic maps is done using conventional signs (e.g. blue for water, green for forests, yellow for agricultural land).
trans-unit.target: __Topographic maps__ are intended to be exhaustive, including elements fundamental to localisation (localities, road networks, terrain, hydrography). They display the physical location of objects in the real world. The representation of elements in topographic maps is done using conventional signs (e.g. blue for water, green for forests, yellow for agricultural land).

trans-unit.source: Examples for topographic maps. :::
trans-unit.target: Examples for topographic maps. :::

trans-unit.source: %% SOURCE?
trans-unit.target: %% SOURCE?

trans-unit.source: __Thematic maps__ display the distribution of specific data or statistically processed information, such as population size, disease incidence, flooding risk, etc. The representation of elements on thematic maps is decided according to the rules of graphic semiology.
trans-unit.target: __Thematic maps__ display the distribution of specific data or statistically processed information, such as population size, disease incidence, flooding risk, etc. The representation of elements on thematic maps is decided according to the rules of graphic semiology.

trans-unit.source: Examples for thematic maps. :::
trans-unit.target: Examples for thematic maps. :::

trans-unit.source: %% SOURCE?
trans-unit.target: %% SOURCE?

trans-unit.source: These two maps use design elements differently. Topographic maps will use symbols and colours out of convention and readability, whereas in designing thematic maps, the symbols and colours you use depend on the context and the information you want to convey.
trans-unit.target: These two maps use design elements differently. Topographic maps will use symbols and colours out of convention and readability, whereas in designing thematic maps, the symbols and colours you use depend on the context and the information you want to convey.

trans-unit.source: Visual Variables
trans-unit.target: Visual Variables

trans-unit.source: You can use different graphic signs depending on the type of information you want to display (Source: Unknown; This figure is included for illustrative purposes only and is not subject to the Creative Commons license of this platform). :::
trans-unit.target: You can use different graphic signs depending on the type of information you want to display (Source: Unknown; This figure is included for illustrative purposes only and is not subject to the Creative Commons license of this platform). :::

trans-unit.source: Visual variables are the __graphical means for visually transcribing information__. The visual variables are __shape, size, hue, value, texture, and orientation__. You can adjust these variables to appropriately represent the data at your disposal. They allow for the expression of __relationship of difference, order, association, or quantity__ between each element, helping to display different information.
trans-unit.target: Visual variables are the __graphical means for visually transcribing information__. The visual variables are __shape, size, hue, value, texture, and orientation__. You can adjust these variables to appropriately represent the data at your disposal. They allow for the expression of __relationship of difference, order, association, or quantity__ between each element, helping to display different information.

trans-unit.source: Visual variables according to Bertin (1967). :::
trans-unit.target: Visual variables according to Bertin (1967). :::

trans-unit.source: :::{Caution} Visual perception varies from one person to the next according to various capabilities:
trans-unit.target: :::{Caution} Visual perception varies from one person to the next according to various capabilities:

trans-unit.source: Physiological (e.g. colour blindness)
trans-unit.target: Physiological (e.g. colour blindness)

trans-unit.source: Transcultural (green = nature, blue = water) :::
trans-unit.target: Transcultural (green = nature, blue = water) :::

trans-unit.source: Using visual variables in cartography
trans-unit.target: Using visual variables in cartography

trans-unit.source: Based on these visual variables, cartographers are able to interpret and communicate information crucial to humanitarian operations. Depending on the use case and the intended audience, you will use different stylings to communicate different data. OpenStreetMap offers several map products: OSM Standard, Tracestack Topo, CyclOSM or the public transportation map, for example. The data behind the maps is the same, but the styling is different. In each case, the cartographers had different goals in mind (e.g., for the cycling map, show the safest biking routes, bike repair stations and resting places) and chose the styling accordingly.
trans-unit.target: Based on these visual variables, cartographers are able to interpret and communicate information crucial to humanitarian operations. Depending on the use case and the intended audience, you will use different stylings to communicate different data. OpenStreetMap offers several map products: OSM Standard, Tracestack Topo, CyclOSM or the public transportation map, for example. The data behind the maps is the same, but the styling is different. In each case, the cartographers had different goals in mind (e.g., for the cycling map, show the safest biking routes, bike repair stations and resting places) and chose the styling accordingly.

trans-unit.source: :::::{grid} 2 ::::{grid-item}
trans-unit.target: :::::{grid} 2 ::::{grid-item}

trans-unit.source: [OSM](https://www.openstreetmap.org) Standard :::
trans-unit.target: [OSM](https://www.openstreetmap.org) Standard :::

trans-unit.source: OSM Cycling Map :::
trans-unit.target: OSM Cycling Map :::

trans-unit.source: In the following dropdowns, you will find different examples of maps used in humanitarian work.
trans-unit.target: In the following dropdowns, you will find different examples of maps used in humanitarian work.

trans-unit.source: :::{admonition} Now it's your turn! :class: tip
trans-unit.target: :::{admonition} Now it's your turn! :class: tip

trans-unit.source: What type of visual variables or symbolisation methods can you identify in these maps?
trans-unit.target: What type of visual variables or symbolisation methods can you identify in these maps?

trans-unit.source: What kind of information has been communicated in which way (e.g. information about infrastructure, natural landscapes, etc.)
trans-unit.target: What kind of information has been communicated in which way (e.g. information about infrastructure, natural landscapes, etc.)

trans-unit.source: What other methods to communicate information can you imagine or do you know?
trans-unit.target: What other methods to communicate information can you imagine or do you know?

trans-unit.source: You can also use maps from you encountered in your work or daily life.
trans-unit.target: You can also use maps from you encountered in your work or daily life.

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} __Map Example 1__
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} __Map Example 1__

trans-unit.source: Flood affected areas and roads in the Somali Region, Ethiopia (Source: OCHA). :::
trans-unit.target: Flood affected areas and roads in the Somali Region, Ethiopia (Source: OCHA). :::

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} __Map Example 2__
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} __Map Example 2__

trans-unit.source: Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs), 30 September 2024 (Source: [UNHCR](https://reliefweb.int/map/sudan/regional-bureau-east-horn-africa-and-great-lakes-region-internally-displaced-persons-idps-30-september-2024)). :::
trans-unit.target: Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs), 30 September 2024 (Source: [UNHCR](https://reliefweb.int/map/sudan/regional-bureau-east-horn-africa-and-great-lakes-region-internally-displaced-persons-idps-30-september-2024)). :::

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} __Map Example 3__
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} __Map Example 3__

trans-unit.source: South Sudan: Humanitarian Situation Monitoring, April-May 2024 - Damaged shelters (Source: [REACH](https://repository.impact-initiatives.org/document/impact/897badb8/REACH_SSD_Map_HSM_AprilMay2024_DamagedShelters_June2024-1.pdf)). :::
trans-unit.target: South Sudan: Humanitarian Situation Monitoring, April-May 2024 - Damaged shelters (Source: [REACH](https://repository.impact-initiatives.org/document/impact/897badb8/REACH_SSD_Map_HSM_AprilMay2024_DamagedShelters_June2024-1.pdf)). :::

trans-unit.source: ::::{dropdown} __Map Example 4__
trans-unit.target: ::::{dropdown} __Map Example 4__

trans-unit.source: Operational overview or response activity map (Source: [Shelter Cluster Vanuata](https://reliefweb.int/map/vanuatu/vanuatu-tropical-cyclone-lola-distribution-and-gap-map-malampa-13022024)). :::
trans-unit.target: Operational overview or response activity map (Source: [Shelter Cluster Vanuata](https://reliefweb.int/map/vanuatu/vanuatu-tropical-cyclone-lola-distribution-and-gap-map-malampa-13022024)). :::

trans-unit.source: Choropleth & Graduated Symbol Maps
trans-unit.target: Choropleth & Graduated Symbol Maps

trans-unit.source: Choropleth and Graduated symbol maps are two common thematic map types used in humanitarian work.
trans-unit.target: Choropleth and Graduated symbol maps are two common thematic map types used in humanitarian work.

trans-unit.source: In essence:
trans-unit.target: In essence:

trans-unit.source: Data is divided into areas on the map.
trans-unit.target: Data is divided into areas on the map.

trans-unit.source: __Choropleth maps__ are maps that show data using colors or shading within specific geographic areas, like countries, states, or counties. It helps to visualize data patterns or distributions across regions. For example, a choropleth map could show population density, where darker shades indicate higher densities and lighter shades indicate lower densities.
trans-unit.target: __Choropleth maps__ are maps that show data using colors or shading within specific geographic areas, like countries, states, or counties. It helps to visualize data patterns or distributions across regions. For example, a choropleth map could show population density, where darker shades indicate higher densities and lighter shades indicate lower densities.

trans-unit.source: __Graduated Symbol maps__ use circles or other symbols of varying sizes to represent data values across different locations. The larger the circle, the higher the data value it represents. This makes it useful for showing quantities or comparing values across different points on a map.
trans-unit.target: __Graduated Symbol maps__ use circles or other symbols of varying sizes to represent data values across different locations. The larger the circle, the higher the data value it represents. This makes it useful for showing quantities or comparing values across different points on a map.

trans-unit.source: For choropleth maps, colours or shades represent different values for each area. Usually, the darker or more intense colour signifies higher values. The effectiveness of a choropleth map is dependent on the __colouring scheme__.
trans-unit.target: For choropleth maps, colours or shades represent different values for each area. Usually, the darker or more intense colour signifies higher values. The effectiveness of a choropleth map is dependent on the __colouring scheme__.

trans-unit.source: Choropleth maps are usually created by [classifying](/content/Module_3/en_qgis_data_classification.md) geodata into distinct groups, either using categorised or graduated classification.
trans-unit.target: Choropleth maps are usually created by [classifying](/content/Module_3/en_qgis_data_classification.md) geodata into distinct groups, either using categorised or graduated classification.

trans-unit.source: Graduated symbols maps are created by changing the size of a symbol in relation to a value in the attribute table.
trans-unit.target: Graduated symbols maps are created by changing the size of a symbol in relation to a value in the attribute table.

trans-unit.source: Choropleth maps and graduated symbol maps are ideal for showing patterns over large areas but should be used carefully, as they don’t show exact values within each region, just an overall gradient or level of intensity and they are used in almost every application of mapping and GIS.
trans-unit.target: Choropleth maps and graduated symbol maps are ideal for showing patterns over large areas but should be used carefully, as they don’t show exact values within each region, just an overall gradient or level of intensity and they are used in almost every application of mapping and GIS.

trans-unit.source: An example of a choropleth map (Source: [AxisMaps](https://www.axismaps.com/guide/choropleth)). :::
trans-unit.target: An example of a choropleth map (Source: [AxisMaps](https://www.axismaps.com/guide/choropleth)). :::

trans-unit.source: Use cases
trans-unit.target: Use cases

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} __Humanitarian Action__
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} __Humanitarian Action__

trans-unit.source: In humanitarian action, choropleth maps or graduated symbol maps are used to:
trans-unit.target: In humanitarian action, choropleth maps or graduated symbol maps are used to:

trans-unit.source: Identify vulnerable regions: Maps can show areas with high poverty rates, conflict, or disaster impact, helping responders prioritize regions most in need of aid.
trans-unit.target: Identify vulnerable regions: Maps can show areas with high poverty rates, conflict, or disaster impact, helping responders prioritize regions most in need of aid.

trans-unit.source: Track disease outbreaks: For instance, during an epidemic, you can show areas with high infection rates, helping to direct medical resources and prevent further spread.
trans-unit.target: Track disease outbreaks: For instance, during an epidemic, you can show areas with high infection rates, helping to direct medical resources and prevent further spread.

trans-unit.source: Monitor food insecurity and famine risks: Maps that illustrate food scarcity help humanitarian organizations focus on regions where food aid is needed the most.
trans-unit.target: Monitor food insecurity and famine risks: Maps that illustrate food scarcity help humanitarian organizations focus on regions where food aid is needed the most.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} __Environmental Science__
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} __Environmental Science__

trans-unit.source: Show pollution levels: Depict air or water quality across regions, helping to identify polluted areas.
trans-unit.target: Show pollution levels: Depict air or water quality across regions, helping to identify polluted areas.

trans-unit.source: Track deforestation: Maps highlighting forest coverage changes make it easy to spot deforestation trends.
trans-unit.target: Track deforestation: Maps highlighting forest coverage changes make it easy to spot deforestation trends.

trans-unit.source: Climate impact: Regions prone to temperature rise or extreme weather can be highlighted to aid climate adaptation efforts.
trans-unit.target: Climate impact: Regions prone to temperature rise or extreme weather can be highlighted to aid climate adaptation efforts.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} __Public Health__
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} __Public Health__

trans-unit.source: Visualize health disparities: Show regions with high rates of disease, poor access to healthcare, or varying vaccination rates.
trans-unit.target: Visualize health disparities: Show regions with high rates of disease, poor access to healthcare, or varying vaccination rates.

trans-unit.source: Resource allocation: Health authorities use these maps to allocate medical resources based on areas with higher health needs.
trans-unit.target: Resource allocation: Health authorities use these maps to allocate medical resources based on areas with higher health needs.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} __Urban Planning and Infrastructure__
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} __Urban Planning and Infrastructure__

trans-unit.source: Display population density: Maps show where populations are concentrated, helping in city planning and infrastructure development.
trans-unit.target: Display population density: Maps show where populations are concentrated, helping in city planning and infrastructure development.

trans-unit.source: Identify socioeconomic patterns: Urban planners use these maps to see income, employment, and education levels across neighborhoods.
trans-unit.target: Identify socioeconomic patterns: Urban planners use these maps to see income, employment, and education levels across neighborhoods.

trans-unit.source: Symbology and styling
trans-unit.target: Symbology and styling

trans-unit.source: Depending on the use case and type of geodata at your disposal, there are multiple ways to visualise geodata in a comprehensive format. For example, you can:
trans-unit.target: Depending on the use case and type of geodata at your disposal, there are multiple ways to visualise geodata in a comprehensive format. For example, you can:

trans-unit.source: You can change the 'styling' and colour of the data.
trans-unit.target: You can change the 'styling' and colour of the data.

trans-unit.source: Change the thickness, colour of lines, or assign a dash pattern.
trans-unit.target: Change the thickness, colour of lines, or assign a dash pattern.

trans-unit.source: Change the colour and outlines of polygons.
trans-unit.target: Change the colour and outlines of polygons.

trans-unit.source: You can assign symbols or pictures to geodata.
trans-unit.target: You can assign symbols or pictures to geodata.

trans-unit.source: Change the size, colour and orientation of symbols.
trans-unit.target: Change the size, colour and orientation of symbols.

trans-unit.source: You can add text labels.
trans-unit.target: You can add text labels.

trans-unit.source: You can change the font, colour and orientation of the text label.
trans-unit.target: You can change the font, colour and orientation of the text label.

trans-unit.source: For raster data, you can assign a colour gradient for the different values.
trans-unit.target: For raster data, you can assign a colour gradient for the different values.

trans-unit.source: The styling of a layer is how you communicate the information to your audience. Each data-layer in your QGIS-project has its own styling rules. These can range from simple (e.g. display line data as black lines, assigning a colour to polygons) to more complex (e.g. differentiate between different types of roads, add complex fill-patterns to polygons, or add SVG-symbols of varying sizes).
trans-unit.target: The styling of a layer is how you communicate the information to your audience. Each data-layer in your QGIS-project has its own styling rules. These can range from simple (e.g. display line data as black lines, assigning a colour to polygons) to more complex (e.g. differentiate between different types of roads, add complex fill-patterns to polygons, or add SVG-symbols of varying sizes).

trans-unit.source: Symbology
trans-unit.target: Symbology

trans-unit.source: Symbology is used to change the look of features on a map.
trans-unit.target: Symbology is used to change the look of features on a map.

trans-unit.source: It makes maps more visually appealing and easier to read.
trans-unit.target: It makes maps more visually appealing and easier to read.

trans-unit.source: Colours and styles represent a specific information.
trans-unit.target: Colours and styles represent a specific information.

trans-unit.source: Symbology is applied to layers, but within the same layer we can assign multiple styles to features.
trans-unit.target: Symbology is applied to layers, but within the same layer we can assign multiple styles to features.

trans-unit.source: The symbology of a layer can be __changed based on one of the layer's attributes__.
trans-unit.target: The symbology of a layer can be __changed based on one of the layer's attributes__.

trans-unit.source: Colours
trans-unit.target: Colours

trans-unit.source: Colours are arguably the most striking visual variables as they are easily distinguishable. However, depending on the type of data and the information you wish to convey, there are a few things to consider when choosing a colour scheme for your map. The most important variables for colours are the __hue__, the __value__ (saturation) and the __transparency__.
trans-unit.target: Colours are arguably the most striking visual variables as they are easily distinguishable. However, depending on the type of data and the information you wish to convey, there are a few things to consider when choosing a colour scheme for your map. The most important variables for colours are the __hue__, the __value__ (saturation) and the __transparency__.

trans-unit.source: Colours schemes can be __categorial, sequential, or diverging__. If you wish to display different types of buildings or roads, the colour schemes should be categorial. Colour gradients, either sequential or diverging, are used for numerical data or data that can be ordered. For example, for the population sizes of districts a sequential colouring scheme is best to show the relative difference between the values. However, if the data has positive __and__ negative values, a diverging colour gradient should be used.
trans-unit.target: Colours schemes can be __categorial, sequential, or diverging__. If you wish to display different types of buildings or roads, the colour schemes should be categorial. Colour gradients, either sequential or diverging, are used for numerical data or data that can be ordered. For example, for the population sizes of districts a sequential colouring scheme is best to show the relative difference between the values. However, if the data has positive __and__ negative values, a diverging colour gradient should be used.

trans-unit.source: Different types of colouring schemes. :::
trans-unit.target: Different types of colouring schemes. :::

trans-unit.source: When choosing colour gradients, a clear gradient from lighter to darker colours is usually the most appropriate, as the gradation is easily distinguishable and translates well into black and white. In the figure below, examples A and B are not good colour schemes, as it is difficult to make out the gradation and it does not translate well into black and white. You can achieve a clear sequence by grading the __saturation__ of the colour gradient.
trans-unit.target: When choosing colour gradients, a clear gradient from lighter to darker colours is usually the most appropriate, as the gradation is easily distinguishable and translates well into black and white. In the figure below, examples A and B are not good colour schemes, as it is difficult to make out the gradation and it does not translate well into black and white. You can achieve a clear sequence by grading the __saturation__ of the colour gradient.

trans-unit.source: Examples for different colour gradients translated into black and white. Pay attention to the saturation gradient under each example. (Source: Stauffer, Reto & Mayr, Georg & Dabernig, Markus & Zeileis, Achim. (2014). Somewhere Over the Rainbow: How to Make Effective Use of Colours in Meteorological Visualizations. Bulletin of the American Meteorological Society. 96. 140710055335002. 10.1175/BAMS-D-13-00155.1.) :::
trans-unit.target: Examples for different colour gradients translated into black and white. Pay attention to the saturation gradient under each example. (Source: Stauffer, Reto & Mayr, Georg & Dabernig, Markus & Zeileis, Achim. (2014). Somewhere Over the Rainbow: How to Make Effective Use of Colours in Meteorological Visualizations. Bulletin of the American Meteorological Society. 96. 140710055335002. 10.1175/BAMS-D-13-00155.1.) :::

trans-unit.source: Colour gradients can also encompass multiple hues:
trans-unit.target: Colour gradients can also encompass multiple hues:

trans-unit.source: Single hue gradient on the left; Multiple hue gradient on the right. :::
trans-unit.target: Single hue gradient on the left; Multiple hue gradient on the right. :::

trans-unit.source: :::{tip} The [Colourbrewer website](colorbrewer2.org) is a quick and useful tool to select and generate colour palettes for your use case. :::
trans-unit.target: :::{tip} The [Colourbrewer website](colorbrewer2.org) is a quick and useful tool to select and generate colour palettes for your use case. :::

trans-unit.source: Colourblindness
trans-unit.target: Colourblindness

trans-unit.source: When choosing the colours, you have to keep in mind that colour gradients (especially diverging Red-Green gradients) can be hard or impossible to distinguish for people with colour blindness.
trans-unit.target: When choosing the colours, you have to keep in mind that colour gradients (especially diverging Red-Green gradients) can be hard or impossible to distinguish for people with colour blindness.

trans-unit.source: Different Colour schemes for the Colour Vision Impaired (Source: Jenny, Bernhard, and Nathaniel Vaughn Kelso. (2007). Color Design for the Color Vision Impaired. *Cartographic Perspectives*, no. 58 (September 1, 2007): 61-67. https://doi.org/10.14714/CP58.270). :::
trans-unit.target: Different Colour schemes for the Colour Vision Impaired (Source: Jenny, Bernhard, and Nathaniel Vaughn Kelso. (2007). Color Design for the Color Vision Impaired. *Cartographic Perspectives*, no. 58 (September 1, 2007): 61-67. https://doi.org/10.14714/CP58.270). :::

trans-unit.source: Complex Maps
trans-unit.target: Complex Maps

trans-unit.source: The different symbolisation methods discussed in this chapter can be combined in different ways to create sophisticated reports and maps. By combining several methods, you can communicate different phenomena intuitively to the reader. A classical example is a combination of a choropleth map (graduated colours) with a proportional circles map (see {numref}`en_complex_map_lebanon`).
trans-unit.target: The different symbolisation methods discussed in this chapter can be combined in different ways to create sophisticated reports and maps. By combining several methods, you can communicate different phenomena intuitively to the reader. A classical example is a combination of a choropleth map (graduated colours) with a proportional circles map (see {numref}`en_complex_map_lebanon`).

trans-unit.source: __Complex Map 1:__
trans-unit.target: __Complex Map 1:__

trans-unit.source: A complex map using graduated colours and proportional circles (Source: [REACH](https://reliefweb.int/map/lebanon/lebanon-conflict-and-displacement-overview-30-september-2024)) :::
trans-unit.target: A complex map using graduated colours and proportional circles (Source: [REACH](https://reliefweb.int/map/lebanon/lebanon-conflict-and-displacement-overview-30-september-2024)) :::

trans-unit.source: __Complex Map 2:__
trans-unit.target: __Complex Map 2:__

trans-unit.source: A complex map combining layer styling and different visual variables to communicate a complex situation (Source: [SIMS](https://rcrcsims.org/portfolio/view/18)). :::
trans-unit.target: A complex map combining layer styling and different visual variables to communicate a complex situation (Source: [SIMS](https://rcrcsims.org/portfolio/view/18)). :::

trans-unit.source: __Complex Map 3:__
trans-unit.target: __Complex Map 3:__

trans-unit.source: A complex map using graduated colours to signify flood depth as well as the flood hazard for IDP sites (Source: [REACH](https://reliefweb.int/map/yemen/flood-hazard-idp-sites-marib-governorate-flood-depth-model-january-2024-production-date-07-may-2024)) :::
trans-unit.target: A complex map using graduated colours to signify flood depth as well as the flood hazard for IDP sites (Source: [REACH](https://reliefweb.int/map/yemen/flood-hazard-idp-sites-marib-governorate-flood-depth-model-january-2024-production-date-07-may-2024)) :::

trans-unit.source: :::{admonition} Now it's your turn! :class: tip {numref}`en_complex_map_lebanon`, {numref}`en_complex_bivariate_map` and {numref}`en_complex_map_example_yemen` make use of graduated colours as well as graduated symbols. What other symbolisation principles can you detect on these maps? :::
trans-unit.target: :::{admonition} Now it's your turn! :class: tip {numref}`en_complex_map_lebanon`, {numref}`en_complex_bivariate_map` and {numref}`en_complex_map_example_yemen` make use of graduated colours as well as graduated symbols. What other symbolisation principles can you detect on these maps? :::

trans-unit.source: Now that we have learned the different visual variables, and know how to use them to create complex maps, the next chapter [Symbology for Vector data](/content/Module_4/en_qgis_styling_vector_data.md) will explain how to set up different symbolisation and styling methods in QGIS.
trans-unit.target: Now that we have learned the different visual variables, and know how to use them to create complex maps, the next chapter [Symbology for Vector data](/content/Module_4/en_qgis_styling_vector_data.md) will explain how to set up different symbolisation and styling methods in QGIS.

trans-unit.source: Self-Assessment Questions
trans-unit.target: Self-Assessment Questions

trans-unit.source: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note
trans-unit.target: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note

trans-unit.source: __What are “visual variables” (graphic variables) and how do they help in map design?__
trans-unit.target: __What are “visual variables” (graphic variables) and how do they help in map design?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: Visual variables are graphical properties of map symbols (points, lines, polygons) that can be varied to encode information (e.g. hue, value, size, shape, orientation, texture).
trans-unit.target: Visual variables are graphical properties of map symbols (points, lines, polygons) that can be varied to encode information (e.g. hue, value, size, shape, orientation, texture).

trans-unit.source: They help to design the visual representation of geodata to communicate information:
trans-unit.target: They help to design the visual representation of geodata to communicate information:

trans-unit.source: use the size to show the magnitude
trans-unit.target: use the size to show the magnitude

trans-unit.source: use different colours to distinguish between classes
trans-unit.target: use different colours to distinguish between classes

trans-unit.source: use the intensity of a colour (value) to show a progression or an increase in intensity of a phenomenon. :::
trans-unit.target: use the intensity of a colour (value) to show a progression or an increase in intensity of a phenomenon. :::

trans-unit.source: __Why is it important to consider colour schemes (hue, value, saturation) when styling maps?__
trans-unit.target: __Why is it important to consider colour schemes (hue, value, saturation) when styling maps?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: __Hue__ is the “color family” (red, blue, green, etc.); it’s good for __distinguishing categories__.
trans-unit.target: __Hue__ is the “color family” (red, blue, green, etc.); it’s good for __distinguishing categories__.

trans-unit.source: __Value__ (or lightness/darkness) gives a sense of order: darker → more, lighter → less. Useful for quantitative data.
trans-unit.target: __Value__ (or lightness/darkness) gives a sense of order: darker → more, lighter → less. Useful for quantitative data.

trans-unit.source: __Saturation__ (intensity of color) influences how vivid or muted a color appears; high saturation draws attention, low saturation recedes.
trans-unit.target: __Saturation__ (intensity of color) influences how vivid or muted a color appears; high saturation draws attention, low saturation recedes.

trans-unit.source: Using inappropriate combinations (e.g. very saturated adjacent hues, or small value differences) can reduce contrast, make features indistinguishable, or cause color “vibrations” or optical illusions. The scheme must be perceptually effective, accessible (e.g. for color-blind readers), consistent, and appropriate to the type of data (nominal vs ordinal vs interval) and map purpose. :::
trans-unit.target: Using inappropriate combinations (e.g. very saturated adjacent hues, or small value differences) can reduce contrast, make features indistinguishable, or cause color “vibrations” or optical illusions. The scheme must be perceptually effective, accessible (e.g. for color-blind readers), consistent, and appropriate to the type of data (nominal vs ordinal vs interval) and map purpose. :::

trans-unit.source: __What is the difference between categorical (qualitative), sequential, and diverging colour schemes? When is each appropriate?__
trans-unit.target: __What is the difference between categorical (qualitative), sequential, and diverging colour schemes? When is each appropriate?__

trans-unit.source: __Categorical (qualitative)__ Used for nominal (no inherent order) data. Each category gets a distinct hue (or shape) without implying magnitude. Example: land‑use types (forest, urban, water).
trans-unit.target: __Categorical (qualitative)__ Used for nominal (no inherent order) data. Each category gets a distinct hue (or shape) without implying magnitude. Example: land‑use types (forest, urban, water).

trans-unit.source: __Sequential__ Used for data that progress in one direction (low → high). Usually a single hue whose value or saturation changes gradually (e.g. light to dark). Appropriate for e.g. population density, income, temperature.
trans-unit.target: __Sequential__ Used for data that progress in one direction (low → high). Usually a single hue whose value or saturation changes gradually (e.g. light to dark). Appropriate for e.g. population density, income, temperature.

trans-unit.source: __Diverging__ Used for data that deviate around a meaningful midpoint (e.g. zero, average). Two contrasting hues diverge from a central neutral color. Example: change maps (positive growth vs negative decline), anomalies above/below average. :::
trans-unit.target: __Diverging__ Used for data that deviate around a meaningful midpoint (e.g. zero, average). Two contrasting hues diverge from a central neutral color. Example: change maps (positive growth vs negative decline), anomalies above/below average. :::

trans-unit.source: __What are some common ways to style vector data (lines, points, polygons)? Give examples (e.g. changing stroke, fill, using symbols).__
trans-unit.target: __What are some common ways to style vector data (lines, points, polygons)? Give examples (e.g. changing stroke, fill, using symbols).__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: __Points:__
trans-unit.target: __Points:__

trans-unit.source: Change symbol shape (circle, square, star)
trans-unit.target: Change symbol shape (circle, square, star)

trans-unit.source: Vary size according to attribute (proportional symbol)
trans-unit.target: Vary size according to attribute (proportional symbol)

trans-unit.source: Use colour or fill for classification
trans-unit.target: Use colour or fill for classification

trans-unit.source: Add outline (stroke) or halo for contrast
trans-unit.target: Add outline (stroke) or halo for contrast

trans-unit.source: __Lines:__
trans-unit.target: __Lines:__

trans-unit.source: Adjust stroke (width) to indicate magnitude (e.g. make majord roads thicker)
trans-unit.target: Adjust stroke (width) to indicate magnitude (e.g. make majord roads thicker)

trans-unit.source: Use different line styles (solid, dashes, dotted) for categories
trans-unit.target: Use different line styles (solid, dashes, dotted) for categories

trans-unit.source: Change colour of lines to distinguish types (e.g. rivers, roads, administrative boundaries)
trans-unit.target: Change colour of lines to distinguish types (e.g. rivers, roads, administrative boundaries)

trans-unit.source: __Polygons:__
trans-unit.target: __Polygons:__

trans-unit.source: Use fill color (hue + value) to indicate category or intensity
trans-unit.target: Use fill color (hue + value) to indicate category or intensity

trans-unit.source: Use transparency (opacity) to let background features show through
trans-unit.target: Use transparency (opacity) to let background features show through

trans-unit.source: Use strokes (borders) of varying width or colour (emphasis or boundary types)
trans-unit.target: Use strokes (borders) of varying width or colour (emphasis or boundary types)

trans-unit.source: Add hatching, textures, or pattern fills for categorical detail :::
trans-unit.target: Add hatching, textures, or pattern fills for categorical detail :::

trans-unit.source: __What is a choropleth map? How is it different from a graduated symbol map?__
trans-unit.target: __What is a choropleth map? How is it different from a graduated symbol map?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: A choropleth map is a thematic map in which areas (polygons) are shaded or colored according to the value of a variable (often normalized, e.g. per capita). All of the polygon is filled, with the color (hue/value) representing the magnitude of that variable.
trans-unit.target: A choropleth map is a thematic map in which areas (polygons) are shaded or colored according to the value of a variable (often normalized, e.g. per capita). All of the polygon is filled, with the color (hue/value) representing the magnitude of that variable.

trans-unit.source: A graduated symbol map (or proportional / graduated symbols) uses symbols (usually points placed within regions or centroids) whose size (or possibly color) varies to represent the magnitude of data. The underlying polygons themselves might remain unshaded or lightly styled. :::
trans-unit.target: A graduated symbol map (or proportional / graduated symbols) uses symbols (usually points placed within regions or centroids) whose size (or possibly color) varies to represent the magnitude of data. The underlying polygons themselves might remain unshaded or lightly styled. :::
